description: Lint remediation database for Go projects

remediation.summary: Move credentials to environment variables or secure storage
remediation.explanation: Hardcoded credentials can be extracted from binaries or source code. Use environment variables, secret managers, or configuration files excluded from version control.

G115.name: Integer overflow conversion
G115.description: Integer conversion may overflow the target type

remediation.summary: Add nolint if the value is bounded by validation or domain constraints
remediation.explanation: Converting between integer types can cause overflow if the source value exceeds the target type's range. Use nolint when prior validation or domain knowledge guarantees the value fits.
remediation.when: Value is bounded by validation, domain constraints, or is a small constant/enum
remediation.example: b := byte(value) //nolint:gosec // G115: Value bounded by prior validation (0-255)

G117.name: Secret in JSON response
G117.description: Marshaled struct field matches secret pattern (access_token, client_secret, etc.)

remediation.summary: Add nolint comment if the field is intentionally in the response
remediation.explanation: This warning triggers when JSON-encoding structs with fields matching secret patterns. For OAuth/auth responses, these fields are intentionally included per specification.
remediation.when: The secret field is intentionally part of the API response (OAuth tokens, registration responses)
remediation.example: _ = json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(resp) //nolint:gosec // G117: OAuth token response per RFC 6749

G118.name: context.Background in goroutine
G118.description: Goroutine uses context.Background/TODO while request-scoped context is available

remediation.summary: Add nolint if context.Background is intentional (e.g., shutdown handlers)
remediation.explanation: Using context.Background in a goroutine when a request context exists may indicate a bug. However, some cases require a fresh context, such as graceful shutdown handlers where the original context is already cancelled.
remediation.when: The goroutine intentionally outlives the request context (cleanup, shutdown, background jobs)
remediation.example:
//nolint:gosec // G118: Shutdown handler runs after request context is cancelled
go func() { ... }()

G120.name: Unbounded request body
G120.description: Parsing form data without limiting request body size can allow memory exhaustion

remediation.summary: Use http.MaxBytesReader inline before parsing form data
remediation.explanation: Without a body size limit, an attacker can send arbitrarily large requests to exhaust server memory. Always limit body size before ParseForm, ParseMultipartForm, or reading the body directly.
caveats: gosec 2.11+ only recognizes inline http.MaxBytesReader, not helper functions
gosec 2.11+ flags r.FormValue() even after ParseForm - use r.Form.Get() instead
Keep local golangci-lint version in sync with CI to catch these issues early

G401.name: Weak cryptographic primitive
G401.description: Use of weak cryptographic primitive (MD5, SHA1 for security)

remediation.summary: Use SHA-256 or stronger hash functions
remediation.explanation: MD5 and SHA1 are cryptographically broken and should not be used for security purposes. Use SHA-256 or SHA-3 for cryptographic hashing.
remediation.when: Hash is used for security (passwords, signatures, integrity)

remediation.explanation: The crypto/md5 package is blocklisted because MD5 is cryptographically broken.

G601.name: Implicit memory aliasing in for loop
G601.description: Taking address of loop variable creates aliasing bugs

remediation.summary: Create a local copy of the loop variable before taking its address
remediation.explanation: In Go versions before 1.22, the loop variable is reused across iterations. Taking its address causes all pointers to reference the same memory location. Go 1.22+ fixes this, but explicit copying remains clearer.

G703.name: Path traversal via taint analysis
G703.description: File path constructed from user input may allow directory traversal

remediation.summary: Add nolint if path input is validated or comes from trusted source
remediation.explanation: Path traversal occurs when user input is used to construct file paths without validation. Attackers can use '../' sequences to access files outside the intended directory.
remediation.when: Path is validated to reject separators, sanitized with filepath.Base, or from trusted config

G704.name: SSRF via taint analysis
G704.description: Potential Server-Side Request Forgery - HTTP request with user-controlled URL

remediation.summary: Add nolint if URL is validated or comes from trusted source
remediation.when: URL is from trusted source (test httptest server, validated allowlist, internal config)
remediation.example: resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req) //nolint:gosec // G704: Test uses httptest server URL
remediation.explanation: SSRF occurs when an attacker can control the URL of an outbound HTTP request, potentially accessing internal services. Validate URLs against an allowlist or ensure they come from trusted sources.

SA1019.name: Deprecated API usage
SA1019.description: Using a deprecated function, variable, constant, or field

remediation.summary: Replace with the recommended alternative from deprecation notice
remediation.explanation: Deprecated APIs may be removed in future versions. The deprecation notice typically suggests a replacement.

SA4006.name: Unused value
SA4006.description: Value assigned to variable is never used

remediation.summary: Remove unused assignment or use blank identifier
Check the error or explicitly ignore with blank identifier and comment
remediation.explanation: Unchecked errors can lead to silent failures. Either handle the error or explicitly ignore it with _ and a comment explaining why.
